Approach 1: Lloyds Banking Group Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much value a bank creates above the return that shareholders require. It starts with what shareholders have put into the business, then estimates the profit generated on that equity versus the cost of equity.

For Lloyds Banking Group, the starting Book Value is £0.71 per share, with a Stable Book Value estimate of £0.83 per share, based on weighted future book value estimates from 7 analysts. On those equity levels, the Stable EPS is £0.12 per share, sourced from weighted future return on equity estimates from 13 analysts, and the Average Return on Equity is 14.62%.

The required Cost of Equity is £0.07 per share, so the model estimates an Excess Return of £0.05 per share. Using these inputs in the Excess Returns framework gives an intrinsic value estimate of £1.85 per share. Compared with the current share price around £1.03, this indicates the stock is 44.2% undervalued on this method.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Lloyds Banking Group Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a practical way to think about value because it links what you pay directly to the earnings the business is generating today. A higher or lower P/E often reflects what the market is pricing in for future growth and how much risk investors feel they are taking on.

In simple terms, stronger expected earnings growth and lower perceived risk tend to justify a higher P/E, while lower growth expectations or higher risk usually line up with a lower, more cautious multiple. So the question is what counts as a sensible, or “normal,” P/E for Lloyds Banking Group right now.

Lloyds Banking Group is trading on a P/E of 14.40x, compared with a Banks industry average of about 11.33x and a peer group average of 11.34x. data.title: ASML Raises 2026 Outlook As AI Demand Supports Bigger Shareholder Payouts
data.description:
• ASML Holding (NasdaqGS:ASML) raised its 2026 revenue outlook, pointing to stronger demand from AI chipmakers.
  • The company reported strong Q1 results, supported by accelerating orders linked to AI focused customers.
  • ASML also increased its dividend and renewed its share buyback program, signaling continued capital returns to shareholders.

ASML sits at the center of advanced semiconductor manufacturing, and the latest update adds fresh context to a share price that closed at $1,481.77. The stock is up 4.3% over the past week, 7.7% over the past month, and 27.3% year to date, with a 1 year return of 135.1%. For readers tracking long term performance, the reported 3 year return is 143.0% and the 5 year return is 135.2%.

For investors watching the AI build out, the combination of higher 2026 revenue guidance, a higher dividend, and an active buyback program is a meaningful cluster of signals. The rest of this article breaks down what those announcements might imply for ASML's earnings power, capital allocation choices, and risk profile as the AI spending cycle develops.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for ASML Holding by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on ASML Holding.

Is ASML Holding's dividend sustainable? Check out what every dividend investor needs to know in our dividend analysis.

For income focused investors, the dividend move sits at the center of this update. ASML outlined a dividend proposal that is 17% higher than in 2024, alongside an outlook for 2026 net sales of €36b to €40b and a gross margin range of 51% to 53%. That combination signals that management is comfortable committing more cash to shareholders while also running a capital intensive equipment business that serves AI chipmakers. The continued share buyback program points in the same direction, because it requires confidence that free cash flow can support both ongoing investment and capital returns.

Advertisement

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ A higher dividend and ongoing buybacks increase pressure on free cash flow if equipment demand or margins come in below expectations.
  • ⚠️ ASML serves a concentrated group of large chipmakers, so changes in AI related spending plans or export controls could affect the cash generation that funds these distributions.
  • 🎁 A 17% higher dividend proposal, combined with continued buybacks, signals that management currently views the balance sheet and future cash flows as strong enough to support rising payouts.
  • 🎁 The updated 2026 revenue and margin outlook, tied to AI driven demand, provides context for why the company is comfortable stepping up shareholder returns.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on how closely actual orders and gross margins track the 2026 targets, because that will underpin dividend capacity. Watch management commentary from key AI chip customers such as TSMC, Intel and Samsung for any change in capital spending plans, as this feeds directly into ASML’s tool demand and cash flows. It is also worth tracking any updates on export restrictions, since these can affect where ASML can ship its most advanced systems.

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes expected future cash flows, then discounts them back to today to estimate what the business might be worth right now. It is essentially a way of translating future cash generation into a single present value figure.

For Steel Dynamics, Simply Wall St applies a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model using cash flow projections expressed in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow is reported as a loss of about $42.3 million, so the model leans heavily on expected future free cash flow rather than recent figures. Analyst and extrapolated estimates suggest free cash flow reaching about $3.3 billion in 2035, with intermediate projections such as $2.0 billion in 2026 and $2.6 billion in 2028, all discounted back to today using the DCF framework.

On this basis, the estimated intrinsic value comes out at roughly $310.17 per share, compared with the current share price of about $194.11. That implies a 37.4% discount, which indicates that Steel Dynamics is trading below this model-based estimate of fair value.

data.title: Has The Market Gone Too Far On Diageo (LSE:DGE) After Multi Year Share Price Weakness?
data.description:

  • The stock recently closed at £14.71, with returns of 2.3% over 7 days and 1.4% over 30 days, set against a year to date return of an 8.5% decline and a 1 year return of a 26.5% decline.

These multi year returns frame the question of whether current pricing reflects temporary pessimism or a reset in expectations.

Approach 1: Diageo Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting them back to today’s value. It focuses on the cash that could be available to shareholders, rather than accounting earnings.

For Diageo, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about US$2.58b. Analyst estimates and subsequent extrapolations by Simply Wall St suggest free cash flow projections rising into the low US$3b range from 2026, with a forecast of US$3.89b in 2030. Ten year projections combine analyst inputs for the earlier years with more mechanical growth estimates thereafter.

When those future cash flows are discounted back, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of US$28.27 per share. Compared with the recent share price of £14.71, this implies the stock is 48.0% undervalued based on this DCF framework.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Diageo Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is often a useful way to think about value because it links what you pay for each share to the earnings that business is currently generating. The level of P/E that might be considered normal or fair usually reflects what the market thinks about a company’s growth prospects and how risky those earnings are, with higher expected growth and lower perceived risk often supporting higher multiples.

Diageo’s current P/E is 18.4x, compared with an average of 16.8x for peers in its group and 17.1x for the Beverage industry. Simply Wall St also calculates a Fair Ratio of 23.5x for Diageo, which is the P/E that might be expected given factors such as its earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market value and risk characteristics. This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a straight comparison with peers or the industry because it adjusts for company specific traits rather than assuming all businesses deserve the same multiple. Compared with this Fair Ratio, Diageo’s actual 18.4x P/E sits below that level, which points to the shares trading on a lower multiple than this framework would imply.
